questions.question: What is the primary architectural goal of an Integration Test compared to a standard Unit Test?
options: To test how fast the code compiles on GitHub Actions.
To verify that multiple distinct pieces of the system (like the Express Router, custom Middleware, and the Database layer) correctly and safely communicate with each other.
To verify that CSS animations trigger perfectly on the frontend.
To assert the logic of a single deeply isolated mathematical utility function.
questions.answer: To verify that multiple distinct pieces of the system (like the Express Router, custom Middleware, and the Database layer) correctly and safely communicate with each other.
questions.explanation: Unit tests ensure the engine parts work. Integration tests ensure the car actually drives when you put the parts together.

questions.question: What specifically is the Node.js 'Supertest' library used for during backend testing?
options: To effortlessly spin up a virtual Express.js server and precisely fire simulated HTTP requests (like GET and POST) against your API endpoints to safely assert their responses.
To generate random Fake Data for users.
To test frontend React components natively.
questions.answer: To effortlessly spin up a virtual Express.js server and precisely fire simulated HTTP requests (like GET and POST) against your API endpoints to safely assert their responses.
questions.explanation: Supertest handles all the complexity of binding your app to a network, making the HTTP request, formatting headers, and strictly asserting the exact response status codes.

questions.question: Why is it absolutely critical to utilize specific Jest hooks like `beforeEach()` when writing Integration Tests that interact with a real Database?
options: To optimize the speed of the AWS cloud.
To aggressively wipe database tables completely clean before every single test securely, meticulously ensuring that lingering mutated data from Test A doesn't accidentally crash Test B.
Because Jest mathematically crashes without it.
To forcefully format code formatting safely.
questions.answer: To aggressively wipe database tables completely clean before every single test securely, meticulously ensuring that lingering mutated data from Test A doesn't accidentally crash Test B.
questions.explanation: Tests must be completely deterministic and logically isolated. If you don't clean the DB, running the test suite twice might cause 'Unique Key Restraint' failures.
